title: Replacing a kitchen mixer tap
subtitle: Disconnect and replace a mixer tap on a kitchen sink using compression or push-fit connections
excerpt: How to replace a worn or leaking kitchen mixer tap, covering turning off the water supply at the isolating valves, disconnecting the old tap tails, fitting the new tap body through the sink deck, and reconnecting the supply pipes.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. Plumbing for Beginners (1918, Traps, Wastes and Overflows).

glossaryTerms.definition: The large nut that secures a tap body to the underside of a sink or basin deck. Tightening the backnut draws a rubber washer against the underside of the deck, clamping the tap in place. The backnut is almost always hidden behind the sink and can only be reached with a basin wrench.

A quarter-turn ball valve fitted in the supply pipe below a tap or appliance that shuts off the water to that fitting without affecting the rest of the house. Turn 90 degrees so the slot in the valve head is across the pipe direction to isolate. Modern kitchens have one isolating valve on each hot and cold supply under the sink.

content.text: Replacing a kitchen mixer tap has three stages: turn off the water at the
under the sink, disconnect the supply pipes from the old tap tails, and undo the
that holds the tap to the sink deck. The hardest stage is reaching the backnut in a fitted kitchen. A basin wrench is the only tool long enough to get to it.

The new mixer tap, sized to match the existing sink hole or holes (most UK kitchen sinks take a 35 mm single hole for a mono-bloc mixer). Flexible tap connectors: 15 mm x 300 mm braided hose with 1/2 inch BSP female ends, if the new tap does not include them. PTFE tape and a tube of tap-tail sealing compound or plumber's putty for the deck seal. Towels and the bucket for the water that will drain from the old connections.
Step 1, turn off the supply and drain the pipes

Turn off the hot and cold isolating valves under the sink by rotating the flat-head slot 90 degrees so it runs across the pipe. Open the tap to drain any residual pressure. Place the bucket under the connections. If there are no isolating valves, turn off the main cold-water stop tap (usually under the kitchen sink or where the rising main enters) and drain the hot at the kitchen tap.

Use the adjustable spanner or water-pump pliers to undo the supply connections from the tap tails under the sink. Hold the tap tails steady while undoing the nuts to avoid twisting the tap body. Once the supply pipes are free, reach in with the basin wrench and undo the backnut. The backnut may be plastic (on modern taps) or brass; it turns anticlockwise to release. The old tap lifts out through the top of the sink deck.
Step 3, fit the new tap body

Clean the old sealing compound from the sink deck around the hole. Feed the new tap's supply tails down through the deck hole from above. Follow with the rubber or fibre sealing washer that comes with the tap, then the backnut. The gasket or putty ring seals the deck joint from the top. Use the method specified in the tap instructions. Tighten the backnut from below with the basin wrench until the tap is firmly in position and cannot rotate. Do not overtighten: cracking the sink deck is possible with brass backnuts on ceramic sinks.

Attach the flexible tap connectors to the tap tails, hot to hot and cold to cold (the tails are usually marked H and C). Hand-tighten, then give one-quarter turn more with the spanner. Connect the other end of each flexible to the isolating valve. Wrap PTFE tape on any threaded connections that are not already fitted with rubber sealing washers. Turn the isolating valves back on slowly, check for drips at each joint, and run both hot and cold through the tap to purge air.

A twin-hole sink (two separate holes for the hot and cold levers) requires a bridge mixer tap with separate shanks that fit through both holes. The backnut and supply connection process is the same; there are two backnuts to undo and two supply tails to connect. Some modern sinks have a side hole for a separate spray or water filter tap; this uses the same method on a smaller scale.

Check the connections under the sink six months after fitting for any slow drip at the tail joints. Flexible tap connectors have a working life of 10 to 15 years; replace them when the braided sheath begins to fray or the body feels stiff to bend. A burst tap connector under a kitchen sink will flood the cupboard quickly: replace early.

attrs.symptom: Tap body rotates in the deck hole after the backnut is tightened
attrs.cause: The backnut is not fully tightened; or the rubber deck washer has compressed and the tap is no longer clamped tightly.
attrs.fix: Reach in with the basin wrench and turn the backnut a further half-turn. If the tap is ceramic, check the torque: most manufacturers specify 6 to 8 Nm. If there is no washer, add a thin rubber washer between the deck and the backnut.

attrs.symptom: Water drips from the tail connection after the valves are turned back on
attrs.cause: The compression nut was not tightened enough; or there is debris on the olive or ferrule sealing face.
attrs.fix: Turn off the isolating valve, give the nut another quarter-turn, and turn back on. If the drip persists, turn off and disassemble: clean the olive and check the sealing face on the tap tail for damage.

attrs.symptom: No hot water at the new tap after reconnection
attrs.cause: Hot and cold tails were crossed: the hot tail is connected to the cold isolating valve.
attrs.fix: Turn off both isolating valves, swap the flexible connectors, and turn back on. The hot tail is the one marked H on the tap body; on most mono-bloc mixers it is the right-hand tail when facing the front of the tap.
